data.title: Allspring California Tax-Free Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg California Municipal Bond Index, for the three-month period that ended September 30, 2025.
  • Sector and duration/curve positioning were the largest drivers of performance while credit and security selection detracted.

Market review

Municipal returns were positive (3.00%) and outperformed taxable fixed income asset classes, with the Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index returning 2.51%, Treasuries 1.51%, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index 2.03%, corporates 2.60%, and U.S. high yield 2.54%. Within municipal credit tiers, A-rated bonds performed best (3.08%) and high yield bonds were the weakest (1.63%). The yield on the U.S. Treasury 10-year note ended September at 4.16%, down 8 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) over the quarter and down 42 bps from the start of 2025. U.S. equity markets were positive for the quarter.

With the downward movement of the municipal curve, shorter-end municipal-to-Treasury ratios decreased during the quarter while longer ratios remained cheap. Municipal 2-year, 5-year, and 10-year ratios of 64%, 62%, and 70%, respectively, were rich, while the 30-year ratio of 90% remained the cheapest portion of the tax-exempt market relative to taxable fixed income.

Net inflows were $11 billion for the quarter, and gross issuance was approximately $157 billion (a 3% quarter-over-quarter decrease), with tax-exempt issuance of $150 billion (a 0.5% quarter-over-quarter increase).

In September, the Federal Open Market Committee lowered its benchmark interest rate to a range of 4.00–4.25%. The municipal benchmark yield curve moved downward, with rates on the short end declining by as much as 36 bps and longer rates by as much as 30 bps. Market expectations favor at least one additional cut this year, though the size and timing of Federal Reserve action remains uncertain. The rate environment reinforces the case for municipal bonds as a stable, income-generating asset amid ongoing market volatility.

Portfolio Positioning - Portfolio Attribution* And Strategy

Duration—contributor

  • The fund was long duration relative to the benchmark during the period, which contributed to performance as yields moved lower across the curve.
  • Municipal bond returns were positive for the quarter. We expect record issuance to continue through the end of the year and to outpace fund flows, which points to a weaker technical environment for municipals. Flows remained positive through the quarter, with year-to-date inflows at $29 billion, though total inflows remain below historical averages. We remain bullish on municipals, because absolute rates represent a compelling entry point, and over the near term, we expect income to be the driver for the asset class. With the recent yield curve volatility, we expect to maintain duration closer to neutral, and we see opportunities in the intermediate portion of the curve and expect to be selective about adding duration.

Yield curve—modest contributor

  • Returns were positive across all maturity segments for the benchmark, and longer maturity/duration bonds outperformed shorter maturity/duration bonds by a wide margin. While the fund benefited from an overweight to longer maturity bonds, we do have a larger allocation to shorter calls that reduced the effective maturity relative to the index. As a result, this limited the price appreciation as the market rallied.
  • The municipal benchmark curve shifted downward during the third quarter, with rates falling by 19–36 bps across the curve, and the spread of 186 bps between the 1-year and 30-year Municipal Market Data (MMD) tightened, down from 197 bps at the beginning of the quarter. We remain attentive to pockets of weakness or opportunity that may arise. With only 24 bps between 20-year and 30-year MMD, the continued compression makes extremely long-dated exposure less attractive for investors. In our view, the risk/reward trade-off strongly favors taking a more measured approach to curve allocations.

Quality—detractor

  • Returns were positive across the credit spectrum but were somewhat mixed during the period. AAA and A were the top-performing rating categories, and AA and BBB were the bottom-performing rating categories. The fund remains overweight A/BBB and underweight AAA/AA.
  • Credit spreads remain tight. Despite the rate volatility, the spread between AAA and high yield credits remains near the tightest levels over the past two years, making credit relatively more expensive. Looking ahead, we believe credit fundamentals are well supported by the economy, though the possibility of credit spread widening remains ever present. There are opportunities for more risk-taking investors to explore the high yield space; however, as the July default by Brightline Florida demonstrates, security selection remains crucial in this segment of the market.

Sector—contributor

  • We continue to be underweight general obligation (GO) bonds and overweight revenue bonds. Controlling for duration, curve, and quality allocation, contributors to relative performance included an overweight allocation to the housing sector as well as underweight allocations to the water/sewer and state GO sectors. Detractors included our underweight allocation to the local GO sector and overweight to the special tax sector.
  • In times of economic uncertainty, GO bonds backed by strong income and sales tax receipts, along with essential services provided by municipalities, are generally more defensive than discretionary sectors. We remain focused on these areas while also seeking opportunities in higher-beta revenue sectors where price dislocations create value. These dynamics underscore the need for an experienced team with institutional bandwidth to navigate an evolving market.

Issue selection—detractor

  • Controlling for duration, curve, quality, and sector allocation effects, security selection was negative for the quarter. Relative to the benchmark, the largest contribution effects were seen in the state GO, electric, and industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue sectors. Selection within the local GO, leasing, and education sectors was negative.
  • The volatility observed in the first nine months of the year serves as a reminder to bond investors that municipals are credit products and not just a rates asset class. Security selection remains critical, as certain credits may diverge from the broader market due to forced selling, liquidity constraints, and structural factors. These inefficiencies can lead to atypical execution and present opportunities for strong risk-adjusted returns among liquidity providers. Prudent liquidity management has been a hallmark of our investment process, enabling us to capitalize on ongoing market dislocations while identifying compelling relative value opportunities.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The Bloomberg California Municipal Bond Index is the California component of the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, an unmanaged index composed of long-term tax-exempt bonds with a minimum credit rating of Baa. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is an unmanaged index composed of long-term tax-exempt bonds with a minimum credit rating of Baa. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based benchmark that measures the investment-grade, U.S.-dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, mortgage-backed securities (agency fixed-rate and hybrid adjustable-rate mortgage pass-throughs), asset-backed securities, and commercial mortgage-backed securities. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Bloomberg Taxable Municipal Bond Index tracks the performance of U.S. municipal bonds that are not exempt from federal income tax. This index represents a diversified, investment-grade portion of the U.S. municipal bond market, covering issuers such as state and local governments and their agencies. These bonds are distinct from the more common tax-exempt municipal bonds because they are subject to taxation. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Definition of Terms:

30-day SEC yield: The 30-day SEC yield is calculated with a standardized formula mandated by the SEC. The formula is based on maximum offering price per share and includes the effect of any fee waivers. Without waivers, yields would be reduced. The 30-day unsubsidized SEC yield does not reflect waivers in effect. A fund's actual distribution rate will differ from the SEC yield, and any income distributions from the fund may be higher or lower than the SEC yield.

Average maturity: Average maturity represents the weighted average effective maturity. For a portfolio of bonds, average effective maturity is the weighted average of the maturities of the underlying bonds. The maturities are computed by weighting each bond's maturity by its market value with respect to the portfolio and the likelihood of any of the bonds being called. In a pool of mortgages, this would also account for the likelihood of prepayments on the mortgages.

Alpha: measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta: measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Credit-quality ratings: Credit-quality ratings apply to underlying holdings of the fund and not the fund itself. Standard & Poor's and Fitch rate the creditworthiness of bonds from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Standard & Poor's rates the creditworthiness of short-term notes from SP-1 (highest) to SP-3 (lowest). Ratings from A to CCC may be modified by the addition of a plus (+) or minus (-) sign to show relative standing within the rating categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of bonds from Aaa (highest) to CC (lowest). Ratings Aa to B may be modified by the addition of a number 1 (highest) to 3 (lowest) to show relative standing within the ratings categories. Moody's rates the creditworthiness of short-term U.S. tax-exempt municipal securities from MIG-1/VMIG-1 (highest) to SG (lowest). Credit quality and credit-quality ratings are subject to change.

Duration: Duration is a measurement of the sensitivity of a bond's price to changes in Treasury yields. A fund's duration is the weighted average of duration of the bonds in the portfolio. Duration should be interpreted as the approximate change in a bond's (or fund's) price for a 100-basis-point change in Treasury yields. Duration is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Yield curve: The yield curve is a graphical representation of fixed-income security yields (usually U.S. Treasuries) at their respective maturities, starting with the shortest time to maturity and sequentially plotting in a line chart to the longest maturity. The yield curve is based on historical performance and does not represent future results.

Risks: All investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Bond values fluctuate in response to the financial condition of individual issuers, general market and economic conditions, and changes in interest rates. Changes in market conditions and government policies may lead to periods of heightened volatility in the bond market and reduced liquidity for certain bonds held by the fund. In general, when interest rates rise, bond values fall and investors may lose principal value. Interest rate changes and their impact on the fund and its share price can be sudden and unpredictable. Municipal securities offered by the state of California and its subdivisions, authorities and corporations are subject to the risk of unfavorable developments occurring in the state, such as the inability of the issuer to repay the obligation, the possibility of future tax and legislative changes and other factors, that may adversely impact the liquidity and value of the municipal securities in which the fund invests. A portion of the fund's income may be subject to federal, state, and/or local income taxes or the alternative minimum tax. Any capital gains distributions may be taxable. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled municipal California long funds, the California Tax-Free Fund received 3 stars among 104 funds, 3 stars among 103 funds, and 3 stars among 80 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. The Morningstar Rating is for the Institutional Class only; other classes may have different performance characteristics.

The Morningstar absolute ranking is based on the fund's total return rank relative to all funds that have the same category for the same time period. Morningstar rankings do not include the effect of sales charges. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: United States Antimony: From Obscure Micro-Cap To Strategic U.S. Antimony Supplier
data.description:
United States Antimony Corporation (UAMY) sits in an interesting spot with mining operations for a critical mineral and the geopolitics of today. Antimony is a niche mineral, but it plays an important role in modern industrial and military applications. It is also formally recognized as a critical mineral by the U.S. government. Global supply is highly concentrated in China and Russia, making domestic processing capability strategically valuable. That backdrop alone has materially changed how the market views the company.

I think the demand for domestic mining and processing of critical minerals will only continue to go up. I have not had any exposure to mining companies or critical mineral companies in my portfolio. I have often avoided these due to the commodity trading aspect of the business. Even if the business is well run, it can be at the mercy of pricing on the mineral. That being said, I have been looking for opportunities within the space, as I do think it will only become more and more important to have domestic mining and processing.

What makes the story compelling today is that this is no longer just a theoretical national-security narrative. The company has already secured a sole-source contract with the U.S. Defense Logistics Agency (DLA) to supply antimony metal ingots to replenish the National Defense Stockpile. This contract, combined with surging antimony prices and rapidly expanding revenues, suggests US Antimony has moved into a new phase of its lifecycle. The company has capitalized on the opportunity in the market and expanded operations.

At the same time, this remains a micro-cap company aggressively reinvesting capital, expanding capacity, and acquiring mining claims without established reserves. As a result, the company represents a high-risk, high-reward opportunity, where execution over the next several years will determine whether today’s momentum translates into durable shareholder value.

Company Overview

United States Antimony Corporation is the only domestic, permitted processor of antimony products in the United States, a distinction that underpins much of the company’s strategic relevance. The business focuses on processing antimony ore into higher-value finished products, including antimony oxide, antimony metal ingots, and antimony trisulfide. These products are used across defense, ammunition, flame retardants, energy storage, and industrial manufacturing.

The company operates primarily through two segments. The antimony segment is the core business and includes processing facilities in Montana and Mexico, where ore is refined to purity levels as high as 99.65%. This vertical processing capability differentiates US Antimony from most peers, particularly outside China and Russia. The second segment is zeolite, operated through Bear River Zeolite in Idaho. Zeolite is used in water filtration, agriculture, and environmental remediation. Zeolite provides diversification and stability, but it is a much smaller part of the business, making up 7.7% of revenues in the latest quarter.

Over the past two years, management has pursued an aggressive strategy to reduce dependence on third-party ore purchases by acquiring mining claims across Alaska, Montana, Ontario, and Alabama. The company recently opened up a mine in Montana to mine its own antimony ore. The other assets are early-stage and not yet revenue-producing, but they represent a long-term effort to control upstream supply and protect margins in a tight commodity environment.

Financial Review

The company’s recent financial results reflect both the strength of the antimony market and rapid expansion. Revenue growth has been dramatic. In the third quarter of 2025, UAMY reported approximately $8.7 million in total revenue, up from roughly $2.6 million in the prior-year quarter. For the first nine months of 2025, revenue increased to $26.23 million, representing a 182% increase year over year. Antimony sales were up to $23.57 million for the first nine months, up by 235% year over year. Antimony will continue to be the growth driver going forward.

This growth was driven primarily by pricing rather than volume. Average realized antimony prices increased more than 300% year over year, reflecting global supply constraints and heightened demand from industrial and defense customers. While sales volumes were relatively stable, the pricing environment drove revenues. It also helped the gross margin, as it increased to 28% for the first nine months of the year. This is up 4% from the prior year.

Despite this growth, the company operated at a loss for the quarter as the company absorbed higher labor costs, plant expansion expenses, exploration spending, and depreciation associated with new capital investments. The company posted an operating loss of $4.9 million in Q3 and $4.5 million for the first nine months of the year. The biggest increase in the operating expenses was for salaries and benefits. This line item was $4.9 million for the quarter and only $7.3 for the first nine months. This shows the ramp-up in operations and hiring. The lag between revenues and the cost of new employees is to be expected. The company will see revenues drastically increase next quarter and next year to offset the new employee costs. While the loss seems large on a percentage basis, it is small in the dollar figure amount, as the company is only beginning its growth.

From a balance-sheet perspective, UAMY is in a good position as it looks to grow. This has come at the cost of some dilution, but I consider it necessary for future expansion and growth. The company has raised approximately $66 million in equity capital during 2025. The company currently has approximately $38.5 million in cash and equivalents. Capital expenditures have been substantial, totaling more than $16 million year-to-date, largely focused on expanding antimony processing capacity as well as purchasing mineral rights. This spending has pressured near-term earnings but provides the infrastructure needed to support larger contracts and sustained production. It also provides future potential expansion with mineral rights. While it does have a decent cash position at the moment, it has a lot of potential for expansion. I do expect to see further cash raises in the future to expand smelting operations as well as build out new mining operations.

Outlook

The outlook for US Antimony is very strong. The company provided 2025 revenue guidance of $40-$43 million. That would be revenue of nearly $17 million on the high end for Q4 alone. It would also be growth of around 175% year over year. The company noted that revenue during the month of October was $5.6 million, so it was well on pace to hit the $17 million estimate. The company is seeing growth explode due to high antimony prices and also increasing volumes. Management provided a 2026 revenue estimate of $125 million. That would be approximately 200% revenue growth on a year-over-year basis. The strong growth is only expected to pick up in 2026.

The most important development is the five-year, sole-source contract with the DLA, which has a maximum potential value of $245 million. The initial delivery order, valued at approximately $10 million, has already been issued. That revenue will be recognized in Q4. The amount of $245 million is not set, as the pricing is determined order by order depending upon market pricing. This contract has been the real game changer for the company. It provides visibility into long-term demand and has allowed the company to invest and expand operations. It also established US Antimony as a trusted supplier to the government.

The company also recently announced a five-year supply deal with an unnamed US industrial fabric manufacturer for the purchase of antimony trioxide with a value of up to $106.7 million. This is as big of a catalyst as the DLA contract to me. It shows there is a demand for domestically produced antimony beyond the government. An interesting note on the outlook is that when asked if the $125 million included this new contract, management responded, “No.” This likely means the company has a good chance to beat those revenue estimates.

These contracts provide the base for the step up in revenues to $125 million for next year. Management is focused on expanding processing capacity, securing additional industrial supply agreements, and advancing exploration efforts to reduce reliance on purchased ore. If successful, these initiatives could improve margins and reduce earnings volatility over time. There is always risk with exploration, and the company must balance growth with capital discipline.

Capacity Expansion

The company has been building up antimony ore in order to meet the growing demand and large contracts that the company has signed. It had $9 million of inventory on the books at the end of the quarter. This is in preparation for the continued expansion of the processing/smelting facilities.

US Antimony has spent $9.2 million to date expanding capacity. It expects to spend another $10 million to finish the expansion and upgrades. The Thompson Falls facility in Montana currently processes around 100 tonnes a month. Following the upgrades, the company will be able to process around 300 tonnes. The expansion is around 65% complete. This will allow the company to increase total capacity to 500 to 600 tonnes a month between their Montana and Mexico facilities. They expect this to ramp up throughout the year and by year-end reach that capacity.

Another interesting part of the production is the quality of material that comes into the smelter. Higher quality material allows the company to produce much more antimony. So it is not only a matter of more equipment and personnel but also getting higher-quality material. If the company can continue to find higher-quality material, then its processing capabilities will go up without any additional capital investment. The ore coming out of their mine in Montana is very high quality and should help increase production.

Further future expansion was discussed as well. Seeing as, based upon contracts and growing revenues, the company could run into capacity issues in the next few years. The Montana facility is capped out on room to grow. But the company owns land and is prepared to expand operations further in Mexico if and when needed. They also noted that they are working on some new technology that they were not going to discuss at the time.

Catalysts

I was planning to combine catalysts in with the outlook section, but there are a lot of different catalysts to review, so I broke it into its own section. Management had a great earnings call where they were transparent about the business operations and next steps. As well as potential future drivers of revenue and expansion. These catalysts are really beyond the two contracts and capacity expansion that were discussed previously.

The first thing is that the company began mining operations at Stibnite Hill in Montana. The mine is up and running, and they are moving antimony out of the mine for processing. Management noted that at the time of the earnings call, they had loaded 35 loads of 16 tonnes each for a total of 560 tonnes. It is not a hope that the mine will produce; it is already producing. This is a huge catalyst as it provides a couple of things for the company. It is the only North American supplier of antimony. The rest comes from abroad. This could help explain the DLA contract. I would expect it means many more contracts with the government going forward as a sole North American supplier. It also will allow the company to greatly improve its margins. Management noted that the margin is 3x higher on their own antimony versus what they have to purchase. That is a massive boost for profitability and cash flows. I think this is the biggest catalyst for the company for 2026. They will be ramping up production from the Montana facility and also increasing the margin with their own ore coming into the facility.

Continuing with their own mining operations. The company acquired seven mining claims in Alaska, Canada, and Montana over the last 12 months. These are not necessarily producing and may never produce anything. There is always a risk in getting a claim not knowing if it will have the quality and quantity of material to actually make it worth operating. That being said, they have a lot of potential options out there; not only that, the cash for most of these has already been spent, so there is not a lot of cost to test for production. During the call, the lead mining engineer went through a breakdown of many of these mines and the status of the operation there. I am not going to go through each here, as one can bounce over to the call transcript and get that breakdown. One that is notable is their claim in Alaska; the company feels confident about this claim. They started to get things going for testing and mining, but winter rolled in before they got too far along. They expect to pick up operations in the spring. Management noted the following about the location: “And we haven’t produced anything yet out of Alaska, and we know that’s going to change beginning in the spring of next year.” That sounds pretty confident. Once again, if the company can get production from that location, they can greatly increase their margins on their antimony production. The company is attempting to become fully vertically integrated. The only one in North America.

One of the other catalysts that came up was an expansion of materials that the company is looking to produce. Two of the prospects in Canada are for cobalt and tungsten. Just like antimony (except for US Antimony now), cobalt and tungsten are not being mined in Canada or the US. The company also now has experience working with the government on critical minerals. It is hoping to do the same thing with cobalt and tungsten. This would help diversify the revenue and limit high exposure to antimony prices.

Risks

Execution risk is significant. Scaling processing operations while simultaneously developing mining assets is complex and capital intensive. The company needs to be able to source enough antimony to fulfill their contracts. The quality of the antimony ore can vary and not meet the standards they need. If their mining operations are unable to produce like they hope, they will have to continue to get more from third parties.

The company makes over 90% of its revenue from antimony. The company is subject to fluctuations in antimony prices. Recently those prices have surged dramatically, helping the company. On the reverse side, a drop in pricing would pressure margins and revenue growth.

Third, most of the company’s mining claims lack proven or probable reserves, introducing geological and economic uncertainty. They call it prospecting for a reason. There is no guarantee these claims will ever produce anything at all.

Dilution is another concern. The company has relied heavily on equity financing to fund expansion. The company is looking to continue its expansion not only in antimony but also in other critical minerals. This is likely to lead to further dilution of shareholders.

Valuation

Valuing US Antimony comes with the challenge of the company going through a drastic expansion process. The company is seeing revenues explode and expects even higher growth next year. It is also starting its own mining for antimony ore to process. So there is not a lot of historical data to use and determine profits. This is not a mature miner with established reserves, nor is it a steady industrial processor with predictable margins. Instead, US Antimony sits in a transition phase, where valuation is driven less by trailing earnings and more by forward revenue potential, strategic positioning, and future execution.

At today’s level, the market is clearly looking ahead, pricing in sustained antimony demand. Traditional profit metrics such as P/E are difficult as the company is at a loss. That is expected to change next year. The company is expected to earn $.2 a share in 2026 and $.45 a share in 2027. That would be good for a P/E of 28 and 12.6. These are very reasonable P/E metrics. I would consider them low based upon the growth rates. There are a lot of questions about these estimates due to the factors discussed above. The company is starting to mine its own antimony ore; it has not produced at these levels before, and there is no history of margins.

A revenue-based framework is the most practical way to assess valuation. The company trades at a P/S of 19 for the current year. Next year revenue is expected to be near $125 million, which I think might be a low estimate, leaving a P/S of 6.32. These are high P/S ratios, but not when considering growth rates. 2027 revenue estimates are at $265 million, leaving a P/S of 2.96. The high growth rates make the higher PS ratio reasonable. Unlike the profit side of the business, I feel much more confident in the revenue estimates, as much of it is backed with current contracts.

Conclusion

United States Antimony Corporation is no longer a forgotten specialty materials company. It has emerged as a strategic player in a critical mineral supply chain that is increasingly shaped by geopolitics and national security priorities. Revenue growth is real, government support is tangible, and the company has positioned itself for a potentially transformative multi-year opportunity.

The company has guided for large revenue growth. There are a lot of catalysts that could drive the stock higher. That said, this is not a low-risk investment. I think there will continue to be volatility in the stock. Overall, I think the strong growth and potential outweigh the risks. I think US Antimony offers upside tied to U.S. critical-minerals policy. I rate UAMY a buy.

data.title: THRO: AI-Powered Adaptive ETF
data.description:
THRO Strategy

iShares U.S. Thematic Rotation Active ETF (THRO) is an actively managed equity ETF launched on 12/14/2021. THRO has a portfolio of 247 companies, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.10%, and an expense ratio of 0.57%.

As described by iShares, eligible companies must have domicile, primary listing, and principal operations in the U.S. or be included in the S&P 500 (SP500).

In selecting investments for the Fund, BFA uses a systematic framework for analyzing companies and seeking proactive risk-management by focusing on theme identification, theme and company evaluation, and portfolio construction. Using this framework, BFA seeks exposure to multiple themes, including emerging and structural trends.

This description points to some kind of tactical allocation in “themes” without naming them, because it is a dynamic list. According to a marketing document, “AI tools aim to identify and navigate a wide universe of themes.” If I try to interpret this short sentence with my knowledge of machine learning, I imagine clustering algorithms (or something similar) creating a graph of themes and weighting companies in these themes. Then, another layer of algorithms might choose the dominant themes in terms of expected return based on current market conditions and quant signals.

Additionally, the fund may use derivatives in different asset classes for various purposes, which makes the strategy even more flexible…and unclear.

The portfolio turnover rate was 45% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the S&P 500 Index, represented by SPDR® S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

Portfolio

As of writing, the fund doesn't hold derivatives: only stocks (97.5% of asset value) and cash equivalents (2.5%). The portfolio is mostly invested in large and mega caps (over 80% of asset value), with a focus on information technology (42.9%). Other sectors are below 13%. Compared to the benchmark, THRO mostly overweights technology and downplays healthcare and consumer discretionary.

Nonetheless, the sector breakdown is probably not typical of the strategy and may change with dominant themes.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, with notable company risk. Indeed, the top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent 47.7% of asset value, and the four heaviest names weigh between 7% and 10%.

iShares U.S. Thematic Rotation Active ETF has a tactical allocation strategy in “themes” based on large datasets and AI tools. THRO is concentrated in technology and in its top holdings. Despite recent underperformance, the fund has outperformed the S&P 500 benchmark since inception, although the gap in Sharpe ratio is marginal. THRO has also outperformed its closest competitor, TMAT, but lags two-factor rotation ETFs (after all, factors can also be “themes”). THRO is an interesting piece of financial engineering, and its track record doesn't raise red flags. It may be a good pick for investors who like AI-driven investments.

data.title: Invesco Quality Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

1 Yields declined

Short- and medium-term yields (1- to 5-year maturities) fell between 0.05% and 0.28%. Yields on 10- to 30-year maturities were relatively stable, declining 0.05% to 0.08%. The overall curve flattened, seemingly reflecting investor caution and uncertainty about fiscal policy and economic growth.

2 Inflation still persistent

Inflation has remained above the US Federal Reserve's (Fed) 2% target amid renewed pressure from services and insurance costs, as well as potential tariff effects. As a result, the Fed may need to maintain a restrictive monetary policy to bring inflation down.

3 More interest rate cuts expected, but outlook has softened

At quarter end, interest rate futures priced in two additional 0.25% Fed rate cuts for the remainder of 2025. However, the outlook for further rate cuts has been tempered by persistent inflation and mixed economic signals.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Structured credit markets outperformed Treasuries but lagged investment grade corporate bonds in the third quarter.
  • Valuations of Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) have become less compelling to us following strong performance. Though technicals (supply/demand balance) and volatility levels remain supportive, we now view their risk/reward profile as balanced and have shifted the fund's position to a neutral stance.

Portfolio characteristics*

Weighted average life (years) 7.08
Average weighted coupon (%) 3.18
30-day SEC yield (Class A shares) 3.03
30-day SEC unsubsidized yield (Class A shares) 3.11

Portfolio positioning

We have shifted to a neutral stance on Agency MBS exposure because valuations have become less attractive to us following recent strong performance. Additionally, their yield spreads relative to Treasuries have been near fair value relative to historical levels.

The fund is overweight in 30-year mortgages with 2.5%–3.0% coupons. We reduced the fund's position in 30-year mortgages with 5.0%–6.0% coupons to a neutral weighting. The fund remains underweight in 30-year mortgages with 1.5%–2.0% coupons and in 15-year mortgages.

The fund also has exposure to select opportunities in non-Agency MBS, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and asset-backed securities (ABS) that are not included in the benchmark.
