data.title: SL Green Realty: The Market Is Still Too Pessimistic While Fundamentals Improve
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

SL Green Realty (SLG), Manhattan’s largest office landlord, currently trades at some of the lowest levels since the Financial Crisis, and despite doubling since 2023, the stock still seems to offer a solid opportunity alongside the company’s preferred.

Especially now, after a solid earnings report released recently and expecting the momentum of their recovery to continue, I believe the risk-reward is overall worth a Buy rating, with a very solid dividend and potential to improve together with the environment, despite near-term macro uncertainty.

SLG’s latest report showed some significant improvements compared to the previous quarter and Q3’24 in terms of FAD (~$75.5 million in Q3), although the 9M level is lower compared to a year ago since last year was boosted by significant non-cash reserve add-backs (didn’t recur), while 2025 came with higher cash deductions for tenant improvements and joint venture leasing costs, in part due to significant improvements such as the “3 huge leases” that made One Madison’s occupancy jump to 91.2%.

Recently, SLG announced that its office leasing volume reached 2.3 million square feet YTD (as of early December 2025), placing them on track to meet their target of 93.2% (vs. a solid 92.4% as of Q3) in same-store office occupancy by the end of the year while also maintaining their pipeline of ~1.2 million square feet.

Not accounting for this improvement, we’re looking at an annualized FAD of ~$283.4 million when annualizing the 9M results, which is in line with the levels from a few years ago, which would place them at a P/FAD ratio of ~11.85.

The market didn’t react that well to the company’s 2026 guidance despite the improvements seen in the 2025 one, as this was below expectations. Still, we’re looking at a decent 3.5% to 4.5% cash NOI growth, while their 2027 guidance assumes over 10% same-store cash NOI growth, which would be exceptional if true, but it will depend on many other factors, as I’ll mention below.

Financially, based on SLG’s latest report, we see a solid position for a REIT, with about $187 million in cash and cash equivalents, while the assets are well above their liabilities. The company is actively aiming to reduce their leverage, projecting a further $33 million reduction in interest in 2027, while the debt remains spread relatively well over the next >5 years, with a ~4.79% weighted average coupon.

As for the equity, we see about $3.08 billion when taking out the total non-controlling and preferred equity, which is not far from the company’s market cap of $3.42 billion, and that’s despite the assets being significantly depreciated and not reflecting their real value since they’re recorded at the price of acquisition, not at current market values—which could improve in the future—so we’re likely looking at a solid discount.

On December 5, 2025, SLG also announced the closing of its SLG Opportunistic Debt Fund with over $1.3 billion in commitments, well above the company’s initial $1 billion target. They did this as a way to capitalize on what they call the “dislocation” in the market, with many NYC building owners struggling to refinance loans and the company stepping in to fund them for very attractive rates, getting asset management fees instead of the traditional rent. As the company says in the press release:

We believe this milestone underscores the strong and sustained demand from a global investor base seeking access to SL Green’s differentiated real estate credit platform and its proven ability to generate attractive risk-adjusted returns. (...) Our focus now shifts to deployment into a robust pipeline of debt investment opportunities in a rapidly evolving market environment.

Of course, distressed real estate is far from the safest thing out there, but this could be a solid time for the company to accelerate their actions meant to take advantage of the environment, as the company is now in a stage of investing ahead of the curve, with significant asset reshuffling since the pandemic, including the acquisition of the remaining interest at 800 Third Avenue for $5.1 million recently. As a note, this fund is expected to add about $13.7 million to the company’s 2026 FFO, which seems like a very reasonable assumption given its size.

Dividends

After cutting it in recent years to adapt to the environment, SLG currently pays a ~6.86% dividend yield ($3.09 per share), which was recently changed from monthly to quarterly. This costs about $236.17 million per year (assuming no increase), meaning a payout of about 83.33%, which is not bad and should leave some room for hikes, especially if the environment improves in the future.

There’s also the company’s cumulative preferred stock (SLG.PR.I) with a 6.5% initial dividend rate that’s currently paying a 7.55% yield, with a par value of $25 per share compared to the current $21.62. This has been callable since 2017, but the company didn’t really find it necessary to settle it so far, and right now, with the stock “costing” the equivalent of a ~7% yield, it wouldn’t make sense to redeem the preferred over the common. And even if they do that for some reason, we’d still get a ~16% profit plus any dividends, so this looks like a solid one to consider as well for a safer alternative.

Risks & Opportunities

Currently, SLG is facing several major issues that stem from their asset class and business model. First of all, we have the classic issues caused by high interest rates, forcing the company and its tenants into weaker financial positions. This is normal for the asset class, as REITs depend on their ability to issue external capital (be it actual debt issuance or equity) to fund their acquisitions unless they want to sell assets, which isn’t a smart idea either in a weak market.

This is why rate cuts could potentially be great for the entire asset class, as they could lead to lower debt costs and a decrease in the risk-free rate, resulting in the market having to “risk up” if it wants better yields, which is similar to the environment we saw a decade ago when SLG was trading even above $120 per share.

However, keep in mind that rate cuts aren’t enough to push debt costs lower, as the market is still showing significant volatility. Recently, we’ve seen the market pricing some uncertainty around the new Fed Chair (Powell’s term ends in May 2026), and even though this role only has 1 vote out of 12 in FOMC rate decisions, keep in mind that this person’s words and stances have a significant impact on the market’s expectations.

If the new Chair just obediently pushes for what Trump wants during such an environment, we could see the market’s opinion get affected even more during already tense times.

Then, we have the specific issues related to the company’s industry, as the pandemic caused some (at least temporary) disruptions due to the stay-at-home trends that made companies realize it could be a good way to avoid paying significant rent in some cases. As I said in the Highwoods Properties (HIW) analysis posted a couple of months ago, this entire industry is facing this kind of headwind, so it’s not something affecting only SLG, which still shows surprisingly good occupancy rates compared to the rest of the industry.

Again, on top of the work-from-home or hybrid trends we’ve seen since the pandemic (unless it’s overblown), the reality is that the market is adapting to the new conditions, with weak occupancy rates being met with new construction in Manhattan (and across the country) effectively freezing due to high interest rates and rising construction costs. This is one of the major catalysts in the industry, especially since high-quality Manhattan space is very limited, and SLG is a key player in the market that’s even “overspecialized” in it.

Then, we have the perceived uncertainty around New York’s new mayor, with the market likely pricing in new taxes, residential rent freezes (SLG has some mixed-use properties), potentially affecting SLG directly together with its tenants that may be inclined to leave if things get too expensive.

However, the market should also remember the power that the Mayor of New York actually has, being significantly constrained by Albany's Governor and the State Legislature, which hold the “last word” (alongside the Financial Control Board in case his policies unbalance the NYC budget) that could act as a significant moderation to Mamdani's proposals—and it wouldn’t be the first time a politician isn’t able to meet their promises.

And, even without these extra layers of protection, keep in mind that the city relies heavily on office property taxes (about 20% of its tax revenues), so they’re technically one of his biggest and most important allies, and hurting this industry would only make it harder for him to meet his targets. If anything, the city should consider developing alternative sources of income alongside it instead of hurting one of its pillars at a time when the industry is already weakened.

Regardless of what the mayor will do, the company’s significant concentration is ultimately always a risk to keep in mind, and it’s something that I’d always want to get at a solid premium just to account for it—which wasn’t the case a decade ago, for example.

There’s also SLG’s bid for the Times Square Casino, although that project was almost dead even before Mamdani's arrival, as it didn’t receive the Community Advisory Committee’s approval back in September 2025. Although the company’s CEO initially said it’s "not completely dead" and hopes that it could get a re-vote, the licences were won already by the other bidders in December, leaving no license for SL Green in Manhattan.

Risks Reiterated

Even though we’re potentially looking at what the company calls a bottom a couple of years ago and now a potential rebound in the industry thanks to fundamental shifts, we’re still looking at plenty of uncertainty. Even if that may appear to be priced in, there’s nothing that guarantees that things will improve, as the company now faces renewed political uncertainty, while rate cuts won’t be enough to solve these issues.

Meanwhile, their debt will get refinanced at high rates, while the stock is also down to levels that still imply very high issuing costs, forcing the company to go for tools like the distressed debt fund they announced, as getting cap rates that would beat their debt costs is very hard in this market—and things can definitely get worse before they improve.

Valuation

For a FAD-based model, starting with the annualized FAD of $283.4 million, let’s assume a 5% CAGR over the first 5 years to take into account a potential rebound that’s likely below the company’s expectations, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and we'll go for a 12% cost of equity since I'm starting with the FAD on 76.43 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This gets us to an intrinsic value of $45.53 per share, which is almost identical to the current ~$45.07 at the moment of writing this.

However, although this model basically estimates that the cash flow should be worth a value around the current level, the company’s assets and interest rate sensitivity can lead to much better prices if they sell them or rate cuts lower the interest, so getting this at a conservative fair value adjustment should leave plenty of room for upside.

Conclusions

I rate SLG and its preferred stock a Buy, as the overall risk-reward looks solid and currently prices in a lot of negatives, while there is plenty of room for improvements given the market they operate in. There are several risks to keep in mind, of course, but the current price seems like an overall good opportunity to get a piece of Manhattan’s largest office landlord and its legacy assets.

data.title: Marqeta: GAAP Profitability Is Priced In
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Marqeta (MQ) is a fintech company that issues virtual and physical cards for consumer-facing platforms like Block (XYZ), DoorDash (DASH), and Affirm (AFRM). These payment cards come agnostic to any bank or merchant a consumer would prefer to facilitate a transaction from. One of the company's goals for 2026 is to reach GAAP profitability. Judging by their earnings outperformance on a quarterly basis, the goal is certainly achievable, and this is one metric most investors and analysts that are following this name have their eyes on. When we look at the 3-year total return chart on the right, we can see that MQ lost 22% of its value in this 3-year period, massively underperforming the (FINX) ETF and the (SPY), which both returned 61.11% and 84.73%, respectively. But year to date (the chart on the left), MQ returned more than 33%, outperforming both the indices and its peers. The outperformance was massive during the second quarter of 2025 but has since slowed down as the stock declined by 25% from the peak of August. On a comparative basis, MQ trades at fair value relative to its peers and sector. Even if management could hit the GAAP profitability target early next year, from a sales multiple basis, I am still not confident enough to place a bid right now, as I don't see any upside potential. Given how volatile the stock has been recently, I suggest if we remain neutral and patient, the market could present a good entry point.

Financial Analysis

Q3 2025 Income Statement

Looking at MQ's latest earnings results, revenue increased 28% YoY to $163.31 million. This growth was bolstered by $155.77 million from the platform's service revenue, $7.53 million from other services, and a total processing volume (TPV) of $98 billion, a 33% YoY increase. Resulting in a revenue beat by $14.90 million. Cost of revenue increased 29% to $48.75 million, resulting in gross profits growing 27% to $114.56 million and landing gross margins at 70%. Adjusted EBITDA grew to $30 million and returned 19% in margins. Operating expenses decreased by 6% to $124.93 million; the decline was mainly driven by compensation and benefits decreasing 16% to $84.87 million and was offset by growth in other items of operational expenses. Comps and benefits account for 68% of overall operating expenses and eat up roughly 52% of total revenue. So this is the main lever management needs to pull for MQ to reach GAAP profitability. Operating losses decreased 75% YoY from $42.2 million to $10.37 million.

On an adjusted basis (when you remove the impact of comps and benefits), MQ is profitable, hence the realization of $30 million of EBITDA in Q3. But GAAP standards require us to report compensation as a real expense. So when management says they expect to realize GAAP profitability next year, they mean that they'll be putting effort into reducing the SBC impact on GAAP profitability. Thus far, they've done an impressive job in that. Net loss has decreased 87% from $28.6 million to $3.62 million, and EPS increased from -$0.06 to -$0.01, beating consensus estimates by $0.01.

Given this growth on the top line and the bottom line, consensus is now estimating that Marqeta could reach GAAP profitability in the second quarter of 2026. This growth was mainly driven by lending, BNPL, and on-demand services. BNPL total processing volume increased by 66% YoY (double the growth of TPV) and by 10 points on a sequential basis, driven by more adoption of pay-anywhere cards.

One fundamental metric I like to pay attention to when trying to evaluate payment processing companies is the take rate. Looking at the net revenue take rate (net revenue / TPV), we can see that prior to the Block's contract renewal, the take was declining modestly from 0.46% to 0.44%. After the renewal, the take rate slightly declined from 0.18% to 0.17%, and it remained flat from there. When we look at the gross profit take rate post the renewal, it has been flat at 0.12%. This flat take rate tells me the company isn't gaining any pricing power as TPV continues to increase. Technically this makes sense, as MQ's clients, like DoorDash, process more transaction volumes through MQ, they transition to a lower pricing tier.

MQ's strategy to improve the bottom line and gain pricing power is by introducing additional services like compliance, funding, fraud, and credit services on top of card issuance and payment processing services. With this effort, the aim is to migrate enough clients to this full-service bundle called "managed by," which has higher gross margins than "powered by."

9 Months 2025 Cash Flow Statement

Looking at the 9-month cash flow statement, we can see that SBC cash add-back massively contributes to the operating cash flow, accounting for roughly 72% of OCF. Even though SBC add-back decreased by 24% YoY, operating cash flow increased 227% to $109.30 million, mainly driven by $17.74 million of cash that was realized from net working capital. This resulted in a positive free cash flow of $7.15 million when we include the impact of SBC. The growth of free cash flow was slightly offset by CAPEX increasing 38% to $23.46 million.

To offset the impact of stock dilution, management has been repurchasing stock from the public market, and as the chart below illustrates, the amount spent on share repurchases has recently been higher than SBC spending. In Q3 the company repurchased 3.2 million shares at the average price of $6.13, and year-to-date purchased 64.6 million shares at the average price of $4.53, reducing shares outstanding by 13%.

Q3 2025 Balance Sheet

Despite a lack of GAAP profitability, Marqeta manages a healthy balance sheet, with cash at $747.2 million (a 16% decline from Q3 of 2024), accounting for more than 30% of MQ's market cap while having zero debt on the liabilities side. The acquisition of TransactPay resulted in restricted cash increasing massively to $234.5 million, offsetting the decrease of current assets to $1.2 billion. Correspondingly, the acquisition added $233.9 million in funds payable, resulting in current liabilities and total liabilities increasing 91% to $636.8 million and $649.2 million, respectively. Total liabilities are still $98 million less than cash reserves; this massive cash balance leaves the firm with enough liquidity to repurchase more shares, which would set a good margin of safety for investors. Only $88 million remains from the buyback program, but I expect the firm will increase the balance if the stock experiences massive drawdowns, as that would present a good entry point for management.

Quantitative Analysis

Looking at the quantitative metrics, MQ was upgraded from a sell to a hold YTD; this upgrade was first due to growth rerating from a B to an A, driven by topline growth performance of 20.19% YoY vs. the sector median growth of 8.36%. Momentum also rerated from an F to a C-, EPS revisions rerated from a C to a C+, and profitability rerated from a C to a B-. Fortunately, the upgrade in other factors didn't result in the stock trading at a massive premium; instead, the valuation moderated from a D- to a D.

Valuation

MQ is among 8 companies in this industry with a D rating for valuation, and in fact, about 56% of stocks in this industry still trade at a premium to the sector median, with only 4 trading at fair value and 12 trading at a discount. On an EV/Sales (TTM) basis, MQ scored a B- as it trades at 2.39 vs. the sector median of 3.07; however, when we look at the price/sales (TTM) multiple, it scores a D+ with 4.12 vs. 3.26. These are not significant disparities from median multiples and where its peers are trading; hence, I think MQ is fairly valued.

Risk

Management stated 2 renewal contracts with clients; one would take effect in Q4 of 2025 and the other in Q1 of 2026. Each with an estimated gross profit drag of 2 points. Furthermore, the partnership of Bancorp with Cash App is estimated to cause another 2-point drag on gross profit growth. All in all, totaling a potential 6-point drag in gross profit next year. The impact here could be a gross margin contraction, which could result in the company missing GAAP profitability in Q2 of 2026, imposing a major risk on the stock price. Another risk that needs to not be understated is the fact that MQ still realizes 44% of its revenue from Block; this level of revenue concentration poses a risk to topline performance, as Block is looking to partner with more card issuance and payment processing companies.

Conclusion

Despite significant topline and bottom-line growth, solid fundamentals, a healthy balance sheet, and massive growth of the BNPL segment of the business, I don't see any upside from where MQ's stock is currently trading. Management has been vocal about optimizing for GAAP profitability; hence, I expect this news to be already priced in. However, given the volatile environment we are in with massive uncertainties from the macro world, I would suggest readers pay attention to this name and wait for a good entry in case the market pukes.

data.title: Beaten Down, Not Broken: Otis Worldwide Is A Forgotten Gem With Upside Potential
data.description:
Introduction

With 2025 coming to an end soon and Technology (XLK) stocks taking all the attention due to AI enthusiasm, it's been fairly easy for investors to miss some solid stocks trading at attractive valuations.

Otis Worldwide Corporation (OTIS) is currently one of them, in my opinion. At this time of the year in the past year, the stock is down over 7% at the time of writing. But they've remained resilient while navigating tariff headwinds as well as battling inflation.

While I wouldn't call OTIS a bargain at the current price, I do think the stock offers an attractive entry point for long-term investors.

Moreover, you also get paid to wait with a dividend yield above the S&P (SP500) average and well covered by cash flows.

In this article, I discuss why OTIS has been underperforming, their fundamentals, and why I think the stock offers solid upside over the next 2 to 3 years.

Why the Underperformance

Up until July of this year, OTIS was performing well, trading above $100 a share. During their Q2 earnings, the elevator giant missed analysts' estimates on revenue by $10 million.

EPS of $1.05 managed to beat by $0.02, delivering what I thought was a solid earnings report, all things considered. However, management's guidance revision is what triggered the stock to sell off.

And the stock has been trading in the mid-$80 range ever since. Free cash flow also dipped, with their New Equipment segment providing most of the company's headwinds.

And in their latest quarter, the segment continues to weigh on the share price despite slight improvements. This was driven by a 20% decline in China, leading to a 5% decline in sales in the segment.

This caused operating profits to fall 30%. Americas saw a 7% decline. But solid performances in their Asia Pacific and EMEA regions partially offset this. Management stated they expect headwinds to continue. But cost-savings initiatives to mitigate pressures, with management expecting $240 million by year's end.

Margin Pressures Weigh on Share Price

Besides cost-saving efforts, the company has also been offering different package options aimed at giving customers more flexibility in costs, pace, and planning.

I think this is a smart move since higher-for-longer interest rates have placed downward pressure on consumers & businesses. OTIS has also continued providing service elevators for places such as Canada, Dubai, Egypt, and India in recent months.

Furthermore, the company's frequent share repurchases have led to solid earnings growth, offsetting declines and margin pressures. In their latest quarter, the company depleted their $800 million share repurchase program.

In Q3, they bought back $250 million worth of shares, which contributed to their 9% bottom line growth year-over-year to $1.05. For comparison, peer KONE Oyj (KNYJF) saw their bottom line decline 9.1% year-over-year from $0.48 to $0.43.

And with the share price currently suppressed, I expect management to initiate a new program, taking advantage to drive earnings growth for the foreseeable future.

But margin pressures, particularly in their New Equipment segment, will likely continue to weigh down the share price and limit upside. In the chart below, you can see how margins fell 170 basis points. However, overall operating margins were up slightly as well as margins in their Service segment.

Q3’25

Q3’24

Operating profit

17.1%

16.9%

New Equipment

4.7%

6.4%

Service

25.5%

24.8%

But overall, the company still managed to see slight top and bottom line growth as well as margin expansion through the first 3 quarters.

And despite their disappointing organic sales outlook, lower base rates should provide tailwinds and allow the company to see improvements, likely in the back half of 2026 and going into 2027.

Until then, I expect the company to see share price volatility and limited upside. Free cash flow is expected to be $1.45 billion, down slightly from $1.571 billion.

But the company's frequent buybacks ensure that their dividend remains safe. Shares outstanding declined from 402.7 million to 392.8 million. Using their dividend estimate, OTIS would only need roughly $652 million in free cash flow to cover the dividend.

Their balance sheet was also in solid shape with only $7.6 billion in long-term debt. They did have nearly $500 million in short-term debt due, but liquidity was robust with $840 million in cash & cash equivalents.

This, their retained capital after dividends, and expected $240 million in cost savings give the elevator giant plenty of flexibility for future investments to grow the business.

Massive Upside Potential

Using the Discounted Cash Flow Method, OTIS' upside is significant, with 32% to their price target of $115.08. Investors shouldn't expect this until the next 2 to 3 years, as margins are likely to see pressures for at least another year. Until then, I believe the company will trade in the $85–$95 range.

To get this price target, I used analysts' earnings growth estimates of 9.7% over the next 5 years. I then use 7% afterward to account for a slowdown in the economy and the business.

However, I do think OTIS could exceed 7% and see high-single-digit to low-double-digit growth in the coming years.

Using an 11% expected return, slightly outside of the historical average of 8%–10%, this is how I came to OTIS' price target of $115.

Furthermore, the stock recently received an upgrade to outperform due to diminishing China headwinds.

And with the elevator market expected to grow by 38% over the next 5 years and OTIS controlling a large portion of market share, I suspect the company could see strong share price expansion over the next 5 years.

Risks

Otis could be subject to additional downside if margins don't see improvement over the next 1 to 2 quarters. Moreover, tariffs continue to impact the company, driven by declining profit margins in China.

But with interest rates being significantly lower compared to last year, I think headwinds should start to dissipate over the next 3 to 4 quarters unless we happen to see an economic downturn.

With the recent uptick in job losses, if this continues, the economy could experience further weakening, pushing us into a recession. If so, this would likely see margins suppressed further, leading to underperformance and a price drop in the mid- to low-$70s.

Conclusion

Investors shouldn't expect any significant upside until margins see sequential improvement, likely sometime in the back half of 2026.

However, I don't suspect any further downside, and the current price creates an attractive entry point for investors willing to hold over the next 2 to 3 years.

But as headwinds start to dissipate, I expect share price appreciation to follow as OTIS moves closer to the $100 level.

And as earnings growth picks back up in the high-single- to low-double-digit range, investors will likely be rewarded with strong price appreciation closer to $115 a share.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Stitch Fix: Fundamental Improvements Ignored
data.description:
The stock market wasn't paying attention, but Stitch Fix, Inc. (SFIX) had already highlighted a business turnaround in prior quarters before the company reported solid growth for FQ1'26. The market sold off the stock due to a focus on lagging indicators like active clients, providing a false indication the business was still struggling. My investment thesis remains ultra Bullish on Stitch Fix, as the online personalized shopping business is back in growth mode after years of struggling to execute a turnaround.

Back In Growth Mode

Stitch Fix reported the following FQ1'26 results a few weeks back, signaling the turnaround has gained momentum:

The company reported FQ1 revenues jumped over 7% to reach $342 million. The amount was the highest level since $365 million all the way back in FQ1'24, two years ago.

Active clients were flat in FQ1 at 2.3 million, but these numbers have always been a lagging indicator based on the last 12 months, not the active clients during any particular quarter. The revenue per active client even jumped further to $559 after growing all last year to offset the fewer clients.

Stitch Fix is finally in growth mode for the first time since the start of FY22, with the Covid boost coming to a quick end. The company has constantly highlighted improvements in fix AOV, up 10% YOY in FQ1, due to changes in fix flexibility, sizes, curated fixes, and Freestyle enhancements.

The personalized shopping service listed the following key data points pointing to growth:

  • Fix AOV was up nearly 10% in FQ1, the ninth consecutive quarter AOV has increased YoY.
  • FQ1 marked the sixth consecutive quarter of improvement in active client YoY growth rates.
  • New clients on recurring fixed shipments at the end of FQ1 were the highest in any of the prior 6 quarters.

Stitch Fix has implemented both a Vision product and an AI style assistant to provide generative AI-powered solutions to interact with clients. Both products provide more tools for the style assistant to work with the active client to ship better fixes, helping lead to better outcomes.

Ignored Guidance

Stitch Fix guided FQ2 results, smashing analyst estimates, as typical of this turnaround period of the last year. The bigger key was the discussion around a much larger revenue opportunity.

The company is now talking about a $1 billion incremental wallet share from the expanded relevance in the activewear, athleisure, and footwear categories, while the market appears to be still questioning whether Stitch Fix can even maintain growth. The consensus analyst estimates have already risen from levels around $1.2 billion to $1.34 billion in FY26 and up to $1.38 billion in FY27, but the market still isn't factoring in much more than 3% to 5% annual growth rates.

Stitch Fix just guided to 8% growth in FQ2'26 with revenues of $335 to $340 million. The current analyst estimates are actually only forecasting FQ3 and FQ4 sales of ~$331 million, actually down from the current guidance during the weak holiday quarter. Management already forecast a return to active client growth in FQ3, supporting solid revenue growth going forward.

The company has regularly beaten analysts estimates during this turnaround. One should expect this to continue sending the stock higher after being stuck in this range between $4.00 and $5.50.

The stock could easily trade at 2x to 3x sales of $1.5 billion, or the equivalent of $20 ($3B) to $30 ($4.5B), while not even factoring in a cash balance of $244 million and a $1 billion incremental revenue target. In essence, Stitch Fix suggested the company had an easy path to $2.5 billion in sales.

Naturally, the biggest risk to the story is that Stitch Fix fails to grow active clients. The company has struggled for a couple of years, and competitive threats could easily pop up with the help of AI to build a similar personalized shopping experience.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Stitch Fix has finally turned around the business. The AI-powered personalized shopping experience is in full growth mode while the stock is still bouncing around levels from the prior couple of years when the business was shrinking.

Investors should use the ongoing weakness to snap up Stitch Fix, with the stock trading at a market cap of only approximately half the forecasted sales targets. The company has fundamentally improved the business to make the online personalized shopping service better than in the past, while the stock doesn't reflect the improvements.

data.title: Amazon's Stock Is Dirt Cheap
data.description:
Despite only a subtle Amazon (AMZN) share price increase since my previous bullish call, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating. Besides its undisputed leadership in e-commerce and cloud, the company also demonstrates strong growth in digital advertising. This thriving business is already quite large, even considering Amazon's scale, and it has the potential to maintain robust growth for longer. Furthermore, Amazon is also expanding into new areas with high potential, such as cloud gaming and autonomous taxis. Besides all these ambitious endeavors around unlocking new revenue growth drivers, I also expect the management to continue cost optimization in 2026, as there is still room to develop. With all these positives, I am sure that the company will demonstrate rapid multiples contraction over the next few years.

Reasons to keep buying AMZN

Amazon is attractively valued if we look at its forward P/E ratio trajectory projected by Wall Street analysts. The estimated FY2028 forward P/E ratio is notably below 20, which looks extremely attractive, in my opinion. Besides business-related reasons why I believe Amazon is highly likely to achieve dramatic P/E ratio contraction, there are other factors, such as a strong earnings surprise record and positive earnings revisions.

I have already discussed Amazon's intact dominance in e-commerce and cloud industries in my previous article. Nothing has changed since late September, as both businesses delivered strong double-digit revenue growth in Q3. This dominance alone justifies a bullish case for Amazon, but I want to share other promising growth catalysts in my today's coverage update.

Firstly, I want to highlight the strength of Amazon's digital advertising business. In Q3 2025, it was the fastest-growing business of Amazon for the third quarter in a row. Another big positive sign is that the advertising business' revenue growth is accelerating. Last but not least, Amazon's digital advertising revenue is growing faster than undisputed industry leaders, Google and Meta. Of course, their scale is much larger than that of Amazon's advertising business. However, the fact that Amazon is growing faster suggests that it is able to gain market share at Google's and Meta's expense. Being able to compete with these giants underscores the potential of Amazon's digital advertising business. Since the digital advertising industry is secularly growing, this business line has the potential to continue aggressive growth over the long term.

Besides standalone revenue growth, there is an apparent synergistic effect, as there is e-commerce business and strong AI exposure via AWS. I believe that all three of these assets complement each other, as AI capabilities help to improve digital advertising efficiency, which in turn is likely to convert into higher conversion rates for merchants across the e-commerce platform. High advertising ROI for merchants is highly likely to create a positive experience for them, which will contribute to advertising customer retention. It is also vital to understand that digital advertising has the potential to become a highly profitable business thanks to Amazon's massive scale and extensive available infrastructure from its core businesses.

Apart from this already established but still rapidly growing digital advertising business, Amazon is also expanding into younger areas with even more aggressive growth potential. The company recently launched its Luna cloud gaming service in partnership with Comcast (CMCSA), which is a new business for Amazon. This new business opens new growth opportunities, especially taking into account that Amazon's Subscription Services business is quite successful, as it continued demonstrating double-digit revenue growth in Q3. With the cloud gaming offering, the company now has more options to bundle its Prime offerings, which will likely help to expand its reach. The cloud gaming industry is a high-growth area with a projected 44% CAGR by 2030. It is also vital to mention here that there will likely be synergy with AWS, as its extensive infrastructure is vital for cloud gaming. Moreover, Amazon's presence in digital gaming might also help attract more B2B clients for AWS, offering its infrastructure for various other game publishers. Therefore, this new endeavor looks very promising as well.

Another new business with the potential to demonstrate exponential growth over the long haul is Zoox, the robotaxi business. Amazon is not just an investor or strategic partner but is the sole owner of this business. This business is moving quickly into a commercialization phase, with early users already experiencing Zoox in Las Vegas. The company has already expanded its test footprint to several large U.S. cities like Seattle, Austin, and Miami. The robotaxi industry is considered among the most impactful technological disruptions for the next decade, which means that it will be growing aggressively as well. With Amazon as its parent company, Zoox can rely on massive financial and technological support, which are formidable assets for successfully competing against Tesla and Waymo. Moreover, I see synergistic potential here as well, as Zoox can be used for last-mile delivery, Prime subscription bundling, or Alexa integration.

To understand Amazon's fair value per share, we need the FY2026 EPS estimate from Wall Street and multiply it by the P/E ratio assumption. The EPS forecast for FY2026 is $7.85, and I consider it very reliable because it is an average opinion of 60 Wall Street analysts, which is certainly representative.

The P/E multiple assumption needs to be conservative because Amazon's EPS growth towards $7.85 is not guaranteed. Therefore, I take the midpoint between the current multiple of around 33 and the long-term average of 57, which is 45. Multiplying $7.85 by 45 yields the fair value of $353. It implies a strong 52% upside from current levels, which means that AMZN is dirt cheap.

I think that delivering a robust rally in 2026 is realistic for AMZN not only because of the catalysts related to long-term revenue growth potential but also due to the cost side of the profitability equation. If we look at the company's profitability performance in Q3 2025, there was solid margin expansion. At first glance, the company's operating margin expanded by just 20-bps YoY, from 10.96% to 11.06%. But it is vital to highlight the fact that the operating margin expansion could have been much more aggressive if the company didn't boost its R&D-to-revenue ratio from 11.7% to 13.7%. Under GAAP, R&D is not capitalized, but history proves that in Amazon's case R&D costs are investments and rarely expenses. It is evident from the company's 21% revenue CAGR and 35% EBITDA CAGR over the past decade. The fact that Amazon's EBITDA is growing faster than revenue suggests strong operating leverage, which is a strong bullish factor. Therefore, I am confident that Amazon's $101 billion TTM R&D expenses are likely to pay off.

With the operating margin expanding despite aggressive ramp-up in R&D spending, we have to give credit to the management's commitment to keep costs under control. With strong operating leverage, we are highly likely to see strong EPS growth alongside the projected 11% revenue growth in FY2026. There is another large but frequently overlooked EPS growth catalyst for Amazon. The company is one of the world's largest employers, with approximately 1.5 million employees. In the era of generative AI and robotics, there is vast room to cut payroll costs. Amazon has been cutting thousands of jobs during 2025, and I expect this trend to continue in 2026. Some sources cite extremely aggressive headcount cut targets as a potential 600,000 personnel cut due to robotization. This might be an excessively aggressive forecast, but even cutting headcount by another 50,000 in 2026 will mean $7.5 billion in structural annual cost savings (if we assume that each employee on average costs the company $150k/year).

Reasons to avoid AMZN

AWS is still by far ahead of its main rivals in terms of market share, but I think that the cloud business is the one where competition risks are the highest. Microsoft (Azure) and Google are the closest rivals, both very strong competitors with their own cash cows that support aggressive investments in cloud and AI infrastructures. Google's recent release of its Gemini 3 large language model was extremely successful, which might create problems for Amazon over the long-term as cloud customers might switch to Google due to its strong LLM offering. All three players are investing loads of money in R&D, and there is no guarantee that Amazon will be able to win this fierce AI race. Losing the AI race might undermine the growth potential of AWS.

With its 4% YTD share price increase, Amazon is the worst-performing “Magnificent 7” stock in 2025. Main rivals in the AI race, Google and Microsoft, rallied by 64% and 15% over the same period, respectively. AMZN's underperformance compared to the main rivals might mean that I am overestimating the strength of its business mix.

Last but not least, despite the bullish forward P/E ratio setup, Amazon's P/S ratio is quite high compared to the company's historical averages. This means that extremely aggressive revenue growth expectations are priced in, and negative revenue revisions, even if EPS continues to grow, might be a notable share price headwind.

The verdict

Amazon is just too fundamentally strong to give up buying this stellar company. I expect the company to demonstrate a massive P/E ratio contraction over the next few years, which reinforces the Strong Buy case.

data.title: Harbor Disciplined Bond ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The traditional investing doctrines may not carry the same weight as they once did. The Fed shifted its focus to the labor market, despite inflation persistently above its 2% target. - Income Research + Management

Market in Review

In the third quarter of 2025, investors looked beyond the tariff-related impacts that plagued the second quarter, at least temporarily, and turned their attention to potential monetary policy changes, as well as threats to their independence. Economic data throughout the quarter painted a picture of softening labor markets and lingering inflation, despite relatively stable economic growth. U.S. job growth notably slowed, with nonfarm payrolls rising just 22,000 in August, far below forecasts, and downward adjustments were made for both June and July. The Bureau of Labor Statistics retroactively lowered the preliminary benchmark by 911,000 jobs for the prior 12 months – or roughly 76,000 fewer jobs per month. However, the unemployment rate edged up only slightly to 4.3%, a historically low level, suggesting we have not yet seen a pickup in layoffs. In September, the Federal Reserve (“Fed”) cut the fed funds target range by 0.25% to 4.00%–4.25%. This reduction, which was the first since December 2024, was considered a “risk-management cut.” Chair Powell acknowledged the cut was necessary as the labor market had weakened, despite persistent inflation above the 2% target. The August Consumer Price Index grew 2.9%, year over year, slightly above June and July’s 2.7% growth. Two additional cuts are expected in 2025, as evidenced by the updated dot plot. The Treasury curve steepened as a result with front-end rates falling the most in anticipation of further monetary policy easing. The 2-year Treasury yield decreased by 0.12%, to 3.61%, and the 10-year rate fell by 0.08%, to 4.15%.

Portfolio Performance

During the third quarter of 2025, the Harbor Disciplined Bond ETF (“ETF”) returned 2.23% (NAV), outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index, which returned 2.03%.

The ETF’s security selection within Financials, Industrials, and agency residential mortgage-backed securities (“RMBS”) contributed to returns during the quarter. From an asset allocation perspective, our underweight to Treasuries was a significant contributor to excess return during the period. Overweights to both the commercial mortgage-backed securities (“CMBS”) and Financial sectors were also additive.

The corporate market showed little sign of cooling. Healthy fundamentals and heavy issuance were met with insatiable demand, despite historically tight spread levels. Fundamentals were relatively stable, quarter over quarter, with leverage close to unchanged and earnings growing by 13%, although margins deteriorated slightly. Investment-grade and high-yield supply totaled over $387 billion and $118 billion, respectively. September, in particular, accounted for $207 billion and $58 billion, respectively – new records for the month of September. Despite heavy supply, spreads continued to tighten, with investment-grade spreads narrowing by 0.09% to 0.74%, and high-yield spreads declining by 0.23% to 2.67%. Lower-quality issuers outperformed higher-quality alternatives, with BBBs and CCCs leading their respective rating cohorts.

Securitized sectors outperformed Treasuries but could not keep pace with corporates. Agency mortgage-backed securities benefited from lower interest-rate volatility. Mortgage rates fell from 6.80% to 6.36% – a level not seen since early 2023 – but housing turnover remained low due to the lock-in effect of current homeowners. Asset-backed securities lagged other securitized sectors, given their shorter duration, but still outperformed Treasuries. Year-to-date supply of $277 billion was in line with last year’s pace. The prospect of lower rates and accommodative monetary policy supported non-agency CMBS. Issuance surpassed $114 billion, 41% higher than this time last year, with more than half coming from the single-asset single-borrower subsector.

Contributors and Detractors

The greatest contributors to performance included SBL Holdings, Aptiv, Ford Motor Credit, Brookfield Finance, and a Fannie Mae pool. The largest detractors from the ETF's performance included a Freddie Mac pool, Navient Corp, Charter Communications, Standard Building Solutions, and Oracle. We continue to be constructive on these holdings and will continue to hold these securities.

Positioning

We strive to remain duration neutral and curve neutral to the benchmark.

Positive contributors to relative performance included our security section within the agency RMBS, Financial, and Industrial sectors. Relative performance was also helped by our overweight to Financials, and CMBS, as well as our underweight to Treasuries. Detractors from relative performance included an out-of-index allocation to Small Business Administration ("SBAs").

The ETF's security selection within the AA, AAA, and BAA credit buckets, as well as our overweight to BAAs and underweight to Aas, aided relative performance. On the other hand, our underweight to the A credit bucket detracted from relative performance.

The ETF's out-of-benchmark allocations to the SBA and RMBS sectors detracted from relative returns.

We invest exclusively in U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-income securities.

Buys and Sells

During the quarter, we purchased Atlas Warehouse Lend in the secondary market at a spread of 1.50% versus a 3-year Treasury. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers.

We purchased FN CB3108 in the secondary market at a spread of 0.29% versus a 10-year Treasury. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers.

We also purchased FN MA4305 in the secondary market at a spread of 0.21% versus a 10-year Treasury. This purchase screened attractive relative to peers.

We sold Foundry JV Holdco 6.15% 2032 as an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

We sold FN FM2971 4.00% 2032 as an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

We also sold FN FS4922 3.00% 2034 as an opportunistic sale due to relative value.

Sector Overweights and Underweights

At the start of the third quarter, the ETF was underweight Treasuries and overweight securitized. During the third quarter, we increased our exposure to Treasuries by 2.54%, decreasing the underweight to 24.27%. The ETF reduced its overweight to securitized to 19.79%, a decrease of 1.53%. The decrease in the securitized overweight was achieved by reducing our overweight to underweight within agency fixed-rate pass-throughs by 2.17%. Within credit, we trimmed our overweight to Financials by 1.59%, changed our underweight to overweight in Industrials by increasing the relative allocation by 1.15%, reduced our underweight to Utilities by 0.30%, while our underweight to non-corporates remained relatively flat at 3.24%.

Country Allocation

The ETF's country allocation relative to the benchmark did not change during the quarter. IR+M invests exclusively in U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-income securities. With that said, consistent with our bottom-up approach, we will opportunistically purchase Yankee issues when we believe they are attractive on a relative value basis.

Over the quarter, the ETF's absolute weight in Yankees decreased materially to 9.75%.

Outlook

The traditional investing doctrines may not carry the same weight as they once did. The Fed shifted its focus to the labor market, despite inflation persistently above its 2% target. Changes to the composition of the Fed could also play a meaningful role in monetary policy for the foreseeable future. Meanwhile, the U.S.' fiscal trajectory is expected to deteriorate further as deficits and interest expense swell. This leads to a potentially very different economy than we have experienced in recent years. As bottom-up security selectors, we de-emphasize aggregated metrics while differentiating the leaders from laggards. We believe that ability, paired with our nimbleness, are qualities that should continue to help us navigate whichever way the market evolves.

Security selection within Asset Backed Securities (ABS) was also particularly additive, especially within subsectors like CLOs and Other ABS. When looking at the portfolio's positioning relative to the benchmark, an overweight's to Financials and Securitized, particularly to Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS) and ABS, as well as an underweight to Treasuries contributed to excess returns.

In terms of what didn't work, an underweight to non-Corporates as well as an out of index allocation to Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS) dampened relative performance.

Risks

Investing involves risk, principal loss is possible. Unlike mutual funds, ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to their net asset value. Harbor ETFs are new and have limited operating history to judge.

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Fixed income investments are affected by interest rate changes and the creditworthiness of the issues held by the Fund. As interest rates rise, the values of fixed income securities held by the Fund are likely to decrease and reduce the value of the Fund's portfolio. There may be a greater risk that the Fund could lose money due to prepayment and extension risks because the Fund invests, at times, in mortgage-related and/or asset backed securities. There can be no assurance that the Fund will grow to or maintain an economically viable size, in which case the Board of Trustees may determine to liquidate the Fund. High-yield investing poses additional credit risk related to lower-rated bonds.

Benchmarks

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index of investment-grade fixed-rate debt issues with maturities of at least one year. This unmanaged index does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment.

Disclosures

All data except for top holdings and performance is provided by the subadvisor.

A basis point is one hundredth of 1 percentage point.

Current 30-Day Yields represent the average annualized income dividend over the last 30 days excluding gains and losses as defined by the SEC. Current 30-Day Yield is the Current 30-Day Subsidized SEC Yield and reflects reimbursements or waivers of fees currently in effect. Current 30-Day Yield-Unsub is the Current 30-Day Unsubsidized SEC Yield and does not reflect reimbursements or waivers of fees currently in effect.

Credit quality breakdown is based on ratings from Moody's, Standard and Poor's and Fitch. In cases where two or all three credit rating agencies have assigned credit ratings to the same security, the highest rating is used. In cases where only one rating agency has assigned a credit rating to a security, that rating is used. Ratings are measured on a scale that generally ranges from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Securities that receive no rating from an independent agency have been categorized as 'not rated.' Certain unrated securities (such as derivatives) are not reflected in the data shown. U.S. Treasury and U.S. Agency securities appear under the category U.S. Government/Agency. The credit quality of securities in the Fund's portfolio does not apply to the stability or safety of the Fund. The Fund itself has not been rated by an independent rating agency.

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company.

Other is the contribution to relative return due to the cumulative differences in carry, rolldown, and convexity.

Shape is the contribution to relative return due to the difference between the portfolio's and the index's duration profiles. The basic formula is the [(difference of each key rate duration between the portfolio and the index) multiplied by the total return of the respective key rate Treasury] minus the Duration and Other effects.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Average Market Coupon: The average rate of the coupons of the bonds in a fund, weighted based each bond holding's size relative to the portfolio.

Duration is a commonly used measure of the sensitivity of the price of a debt security, or aggregate market value of a portfolio of debt securities, to change in interest rates. Modified Duration measures the change in the value of a security in response to the change in interest rates. Securities with a longer duration are more sensitive to changes in interest rates and generally have more volatile prices than securities of comparable quality with a shorter duration.
